exercises.title: AI testing challenges vs traditional testing
exercises.instructions:
For each AI testing challenge, explain what makes it different from testing traditional code and what the solution is:
A: AI response is non-deterministic (same prompt, different output each run)
B: AI API costs ₹0.50 per test run (10,000 tests = ₹5,000)
C: How to test that a prompt produces 'good' output?
D: Testing that RAG retrieves relevant documents
E: How to test AI fallback when the API is unavailable?
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Non-deterministic responses
   Traditional: assertEquals(expected, actual) — fails if output changes
   AI challenge: response varies (different wording, order, details each run)
   Solutions:
     1. Mock the AI: return fixed response in tests (don't call real API)
     2. Assertive testing: check for key facts, not exact wording
        assertTrue(response.contains('HDFC')) vs assertEquals('HDFC Bank answer')
     3. Temperature=0: more deterministic for specific assertions (but still varies)
     4. Structural assertions: check JSON fields, not JSON text

B: API cost per test
   Traditional: unit tests free (just JVM)
   AI challenge: ₹5,000 for 10,000 tests destroys CI economics
   Solutions:
     1. Mock the AI in unit/integration tests (free)
     2. Only call real API in acceptance tests (run weekly, not per PR)
     3. Local models (Ollama): free but lower quality
     4. Record-and-replay: record real responses once, replay in tests

C: Testing 'good' output (quality evaluation)
   Traditional: pass/fail — code either works or doesn't
   AI challenge: 'good answer' is subjective
   Solutions:
     1. Factual assertions: answer must contain correct fact (CIBIL=700)
     2. LLM-as-judge: second AI call evaluates first response
     3. Human evaluation set: 50 Q&A pairs with known correct answers
     4. RAGAS metrics: faithfulness, answer relevancy scores

D: Testing RAG retrieval
   Traditional: test DB queries deterministically
   AI challenge: similarity search varies by embedding model
   Solutions:
     1. Test retrieval separately from generation
     2. Fixed embeddings: InMemoryVectorStore with known vectors
     3. Hit rate evaluation: ask 20 known questions, measure % retrieved correctly
     4. Threshold testing: verify similarity scores are above threshold

E: Testing AI fallback
   Traditional: mock the dependency to throw exception, verify fallback
   AI challenge: same approach — mock the ChatClient/AI service
   Solutions:
     1. @MockBean ChatClient.Builder — control when AI fails
     2. WireMock: stub the AI API endpoint to return errors
     3. Verify fallback response is returned (not the real AI response)
     4. Test: API returns 503 → service returns cached/default response

A: Non-deterministic responses
   Traditional: assertEquals(expected, actual) — fails if output changes
   AI challenge: response varies (different wording, order, details each run)
   Solutions:

     2. Assertive testing: check for key facts, not exact wording
        assertTrue(response.contains('HDFC')) vs assertEquals('HDFC Bank answer')
     3. Temperature=0: more deterministic for specific assertions (but still varies)
     4. Structural assertions: check JSON fields, not JSON text

B: API cost per test
   Traditional: unit tests free (just JVM)
   AI challenge: ₹5,000 for 10,000 tests destroys CI economics
   Solutions:
     1. Mock the AI in unit/integration tests (free)
     2. Only call real API in acceptance tests (run weekly, not per PR)
     3. Local models (Ollama): free but lower quality
     4. Record-and-replay: record real responses once, replay in tests

C: Testing 'good' output (quality evaluation)
   Traditional: pass/fail — code either works or doesn't
   AI challenge: 'good answer' is subjective
   Solutions:
     1. Factual assertions: answer must contain correct fact (CIBIL=700)
     2. LLM-as-judge: second AI call evaluates first response
     3. Human evaluation set: 50 Q&A pairs with known correct answers
     4. RAGAS metrics: faithfulness, answer relevancy scores

D: Testing RAG retrieval
   Traditional: test DB queries deterministically
   AI challenge: similarity search varies by embedding model
   Solutions:
     1. Test retrieval separately from generation
     2. Fixed embeddings: InMemoryVectorStore with known vectors
     3. Hit rate evaluation: ask 20 known questions, measure % retrieved correctly
     4. Threshold testing: verify similarity scores are above threshold

E: Testing AI fallback
   Traditional: mock the dependency to throw exception, verify fallback
   AI challenge: same approach — mock the ChatClient/AI service
   Solutions:
     1. @MockBean ChatClient.Builder — control when AI fails
     2. WireMock: stub the AI API endpoint to return errors
     3. Verify fallback response is returned (not the real AI response)
     4. Test: API returns 503 → service returns cached/default response

hints: Mock AI in unit tests: same as mocking any dependency
Factual assertions over exact-match: AI wording varies, facts don't
RAG: test retrieval and generation separately

exercises.title: Complete the AI mock test setup
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to mock a Spring AI ChatClient in tests.

blanks.hint: @___Bean replaces the Spring AI builder with a mock
assert the response contains the key fact
assert the response contains the expected term

hints: @MockBean: replaces Spring bean with Mockito mock
assertTrue(contains()): factual assertion (not exact match)
Mock chain: builder → client → spec → callSpec → content()

exercises.title: Fake AI implementation for deterministic testing
exercises.instructions:
Write a FakeAiService for testing:
1. FakeAiService: Map<String, String> keyword → response
2. answer(question): find matching keyword, return scripted response
3. No HTTP calls, free, deterministic
4. @SpringBootTest: fake returns expected response for known question
hints: Fake over Mock: Fakes have real logic (keyword lookup), Mocks just return values
Deterministic: same question always returns same answer (no API randomness)
addResponse: extend fake for specific test scenarios

exercises.title: Factual assertions for AI responses
exercises.instructions:
Write AiResponseAssertions:
1. containsFact(response, expectedFact): checks key fact present
2. containsNumber(response, number): verifies numeric fact (e.g. 700, 5-7)
3. doesNotHallucinate(response, bannedTerms): verifies banned content absent
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): fact present, number present, hallucination absent
hints: Factual assertion: check KEY FACT present (not exact wording)
Hallucination check: verify AI didn't invent false promises
containsNumber: financial facts must be numerically accurate

exercises.title: WireMock for AI API testing
exercises.instructions:
Use WireMock to stub the Anthropic API:
1. WireMock: stub POST /v1/messages → return fake AI response JSON
2. Configure application to call WireMock server instead of real API
3. Test: service calls AI, receives mocked response, returns correct result
4. @SpringBootTest with WireMock
hints: WireMock: intercepts HTTP calls (Anthropic API is just HTTP)
@DynamicPropertySource: override base-url to point at WireMock
Error stubs: test 503, timeout, malformed JSON — real API rarely returns these

exercises.title: Testing AI fallback behavior
exercises.instructions:
Test that the AI service fails gracefully:
1. HdfcAiServiceWithFallback: if AI throws, return cached/default response
2. Mock AI to throw RuntimeException
3. Verify fallback response returned
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): normal path returns AI response, error path returns fallback
hints: try-catch around AI call: catch ANY exception (not just specific ones)
Fallback: cached response beats 'something went wrong' error
Mock throws: when(client.prompt()).thenThrow(new RuntimeException())

exercises.title: Prompt injection testing
exercises.instructions:
Write tests for PromptInjectionGuard (from T1):
1. Test each injection pattern individually
2. Test: clean input passes through unmodified
3. Test: max-length truncation at 2000 chars
4. Test: case-insensitive detection
5. @SpringBootTest (5 tests): all pass
hints: Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE: catches uppercase, mixed case variations
sanitize returns null on injection: reject before any AI call
Length truncation: 2000 char limit prevents token bomb attacks

exercises.title: LLM-as-judge evaluation
exercises.instructions:
Build an LLM-as-judge evaluator:
1. EvaluationResult record: score (1-5), feedback, isAcceptable
2. AiEvaluator: uses a second AI call to score the first AI's response
3. Criteria: accuracy, relevance, no hallucinations
4. @SpringBootTest: evaluator logic tested with fake responses
hints: LLM-as-judge: second AI evaluates first AI response (meta-evaluation)
Simple heuristics: length, keyword presence, hallucination keywords
In production: second LLM call with evaluation rubric in system prompt

exercises.title: Testing the full RAG pipeline
exercises.instructions:
Write integration tests for the RAG pipeline:
1. Test ingestion: documents added, count correct
2. Test retrieval: relevant query returns relevant doc
3. Test generation: AI called with retrieved context
4. Test end-to-end: question → correct answer based on docs only
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): all pipeline stages verified
hints: Test each RAG stage independently: ingestion, retrieval, generation
Mock ChatClient for generation tests: isolate vector store from AI
Full pipeline test: verifies stages connect correctly

exercises.title: AI test strategy pyramid
exercises.instructions:
Design the test pyramid for Razorpay's AI payment dispute classifier:
1. Unit tests (70% of tests) — what do they test? What's mocked?
2. Integration tests (20%) — what's real vs mocked?
3. Acceptance/evaluation tests (10%) — real AI, how many?
4. Total estimated test count and CI run time
5. Which layer catches which bugs?
exercises.expected_solution:
AI TEST PYRAMID for Razorpay Dispute Classifier:

LAYER 1: UNIT TESTS (70% — ~350 tests, run in <30s)
  What's tested:
    - PromptInjectionGuard.isInjection() — all 10 patterns
    - AiRateLimiter.allow() — bucket exhaustion, refill
    - FakeAiService.classify() — FRAUD/MERCHANT_ERROR/CUSTOMER_ERROR routing
    - DisputeResponse parsing — valid JSON, invalid JSON, null
    - AuditLogger.log() — called on every request
  What's mocked: ChatClient (entirely replaced with FakeAiService)
  API cost: ₹0 (no real AI calls)
  Catches: business logic bugs, injection vulnerabilities, rate limit bugs

LAYER 2: INTEGRATION TESTS (20% — ~100 tests, run in 2-3 minutes)
  What's tested:
    - Full service flow: HTTP request → guard → rate limit → AI (WireMocked) → audit
    - WireMock stubs: correct response format, 503 error, timeout
    - DB audit log: entries actually written
    - Fallback behavior: WireMock returns 503 → cached response returned
    - Tool calling: mock tool, verify AI called correct method
  What's mocked: Anthropic API (WireMock), external databases (@EmbeddedRedis)
  What's real: Spring context, service wiring, HTTP layer
  API cost: ₹0 (WireMock)
  Catches: wiring bugs, fallback failures, audit gaps

LAYER 3: ACCEPTANCE/EVALUATION TESTS (10% — ~50 tests, run weekly)
  What's tested:
    - 50 real dispute scenarios with known correct classifications
    - Accuracy target: >90% correct classification
    - Hallucination checks: AI never claims 'guaranteed refund'
    - LLM-as-judge: second AI evaluates response quality
    - Latency: p99 < 3s for real API calls
  What's real: Everything including real Anthropic API
  API cost: ~₹50 per weekly run (50 calls × ₹1/call)
  Catches: prompt quality regressions, model capability changes

CATCH MATRIX:
  Bug type               Caught at layer
  Injection bypass       Unit
  Rate limit bug         Unit
  Fallback not triggered Integration
  Wrong audit log        Integration
  Wrong classification   Acceptance
  Hallucination          Acceptance
  Latency regression     Acceptance

Integration: WireMock AI, test wiring

exercises.title: AI evaluation dataset with known answers
exercises.instructions:
Build an AI evaluation dataset:
1. EvalCase record: question, expectedFacts, bannedTerms
2. EvalDataset: 5 HDFC Bank Q&A eval cases
3. EvalRunner: runs FakeAiService against dataset, measures accuracy
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): all eval cases defined, accuracy > 50%, hallucinations absent
hints: EvalCase: structured test case with expected facts AND banned terms
EvalRunner: separates evaluation logic from AI service
hallucination=0 passed: banned term detected → failing score

exercises.title: Contract testing for AI API consumers
exercises.instructions:
Write Pact-style contract tests for the AI endpoint:
1. Consumer contract: POST /api/ai/support expects {answer: string, citations: [string]}
2. Provider verification: /api/ai/support returns that schema
3. Schema validator: validates AI response structure
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): response schema valid, required fields present, no extra junk
hints: Schema validation: separate from AI logic (can test without AI)
Empty citations valid: not all answers cite specific documents
Contract test: consumer defines the expected shape, provider must conform

exercises.title: Complete AI test suite for HDFC support system
exercises.instructions:
Build complete test suite:
1. FakeAiService: scripted responses for known questions
2. AiResponseAssertions: factual + no-hallucination checks
3. FallbackTest: AI throws → fallback returned
4. EvalRunner: accuracy measurement
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): fake response, factual assertion, fallback, eval passes
hints: LinkedHashMap: preserves keyword insertion order (first match wins)
Fallback for unknown: 'Service temporarily unavailable' (default response)
EvalRunner: measures accuracy against known correct answers

exercises.title: AI performance test — latency and throughput
exercises.instructions:
Build AI performance baseline tracker:
1. AiLatencyTracker: record latency per AI call
2. Report: p50, p95, p99 latency
3. BaselineComparator: alert if p99 regresses >20%
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): latency tracked, percentiles computed, regression detected
hints: Latency list: sorted for percentile computation
p99 regression: alert if current p99 > baseline p99 × 1.20
AI latency baseline: measure weekly, compare against last week

exercises.title: Complete M26 T5 Testing AI Apps synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M26 T5 synthesis:
1. FakeAiService with 5 scripted responses
2. ResponseValidator: factual + no-hallucination + length
3. EvalDataset: 3 test cases
4. FallbackService: returns cached on AI error
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): fake works, validation passes, eval runs, fallback returns cached
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M26 T5 Testing AI Apps synthesis
hints: FallbackSvc.ask: if AI returns UNAVAILABLE → use cache
EvalRunner: 2/2 cases pass → 100% accuracy
validator.ok(): all facts present AND no banned terms
